questions.q: Apsara holds the distinction of being the first nuclear reactor in which region?
questions.explanation: Apsara, commissioned in 1956, was the first nuclear research reactor built in Asia.

questions.q: In which year was the Apsara reactor commissioned?
questions.explanation: Apsara attained criticality and was commissioned in 1956.

questions.q: At which location near Mumbai was the Apsara reactor built?
questions.explanation: Apsara was built at Trombay, the site that later became the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre.

questions.q: What type of facility is Apsara?
questions.explanation: Apsara is a research reactor, used for research, isotope production and training rather than commercial electricity generation.

questions.q: The driving force behind India's atomic energy programme and the Apsara project was which physicist?
questions.explanation: Homi J. Bhabha, founder of India's nuclear programme, led the establishment of Apsara at Trombay.

questions.q: Apsara is named after a class of beings from Indian mythology. What are apsaras?
questions.explanation: Apsaras are celestial water nymphs in Hindu and Buddhist mythology, and the reactor was poetically named after them.

questions.q: Apsara was built primarily for which broad purpose?
questions.explanation: As a low-power research reactor, Apsara supported scientific research, training of personnel and production of radioisotopes.

questions.q: Apsara is best classified as which kind of reactor based on the material that slows its neutrons?
questions.explanation: Apsara is a swimming-pool type reactor that uses water as both moderator and coolant.

questions.q: The Apsara reactor was a milestone in the history of which country's science programme?
questions.explanation: Apsara was a landmark achievement of India's atomic energy programme.

questions.q: The Trombay site where Apsara was built is now home to which major institution?
questions.explanation: The Trombay establishment later became the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC).

questions.q: What kind of reactor design is Apsara, characterised by its core sitting at the bottom of an open pool of water?
questions.explanation: Apsara is a swimming-pool type research reactor, with the core submerged in a pool that provides cooling and shielding.

questions.q: The organisation that operated Apsara at Trombay was originally known by which name before being renamed BARC?
questions.explanation: The Trombay facility was the Atomic Energy Establishment, Trombay (AEET), renamed Bhabha Atomic Research Centre in 1967.

questions.q: What was the approximate thermal power rating of the original Apsara reactor?
questions.explanation: Apsara was a low-power research reactor with a thermal output of about 1 MW.

questions.q: On which day did Apsara first attain criticality in 1956?
questions.explanation: Apsara achieved its first criticality on 4 August 1956.

questions.q: The original Apsara reactor used enriched uranium fuel obtained with assistance from which country?
questions.explanation: The enriched uranium fuel for the original Apsara was supplied by the United Kingdom, while the reactor itself was designed and built in India.

questions.q: Who is credited with conceiving the name 'Apsara' for the reactor?
questions.explanation: Homi Bhabha is credited with naming the reactor Apsara, after the celestial nymphs of Indian mythology.

questions.q: Apsara's commissioning made India among the earliest nations in which group?
options: Countries operating their own nuclear research reactor
Countries with commercial nuclear power exports
questions.explanation: With Apsara, India became one of the early countries to design, build and operate its own nuclear research reactor.

questions.q: Research reactors like Apsara are valuable chiefly because they produce intense beams or fluxes of which particles?
questions.explanation: Research reactors are sources of neutrons, used for materials research, activation analysis and isotope production.

questions.q: Which Prime Minister led India at the time Apsara was commissioned in 1956?
questions.explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru was Prime Minister in 1956 and was a strong patron of India's atomic energy programme.

questions.q: In a swimming-pool reactor like Apsara, the deep layer of water above the core mainly serves what additional function besides cooling?
questions.explanation: The water in a swimming-pool reactor provides radiation shielding as well as cooling and moderation, letting operators work safely above the core.

questions.q: Apsara's success in 1956 directly paved the way for which larger Trombay research reactor that went critical in 1960?
questions.explanation: CIRUS, a larger 40 MW research reactor at Trombay, went critical in 1960, following Apsara's pioneering role.

questions.q: Apsara fell under the authority of which Indian government body responsible for the nuclear programme?
options: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR)
questions.explanation: Apsara was operated under the Department of Atomic Energy, established in 1954 to oversee India's nuclear activities.

questions.q: After decades of operation, the original Apsara was shut down in 2009 and replaced by an upgraded reactor commissioned in 2018. By what name is the new reactor known?
questions.explanation: The original reactor was decommissioned in 2009; an upgraded version, Apsara-U (Apsara-Upgraded), was made critical in 2018.

questions.q: The upgraded Apsara reactor (Apsara-U) was made critical in which year?
questions.explanation: Apsara-U attained criticality in September 2018 after the original was retired in 2009.

questions.q: Apsara is often described as having been designed and built largely by Indian engineers and scientists with imported fuel. What does this reflect about the project?
options: India had no role in the reactor's construction
India demonstrated significant indigenous capability in reactor engineering
The reactor was entirely a foreign turnkey installation
The reactor was built without any moderator
questions.explanation: Although the enriched fuel was imported, the reactor was designed and constructed by Indian scientists, showcasing early indigenous nuclear engineering capability.

questions.q: The original Apsara reactor was fuelled with which form of uranium?
questions.explanation: The original Apsara ran on enriched uranium fuel, supplied with assistance from the United Kingdom.

questions.q: A distinctive visible feature reported when a swimming-pool reactor such as Apsara operates at power is a faint blue glow in the water. This glow is known as what?
questions.explanation: The characteristic blue glow seen in pool-type reactors is Cherenkov radiation, emitted when charged particles travel faster than light's speed in water.

questions.q: Apsara's role included producing radioisotopes that were used in which fields?
questions.explanation: Radioisotopes from Apsara supported applications in medicine, agriculture and industry, as well as basic research.

questions.q: Apsara was commissioned only a couple of years after which key 1954 institutional milestone in India's nuclear administration?
options: Creation of the Department of Atomic Energy
questions.explanation: India's Department of Atomic Energy was created in 1954, and Apsara followed in 1956 as one of its first major achievements.

questions.q: Which statement best captures Apsara's lasting historical significance?
options: It was the first reactor to ever generate commercial power worldwide
It was Asia's first nuclear research reactor and a foundation of India's nuclear science
It was a fusion reactor that achieved net energy gain
It was a reactor built solely to produce nuclear weapons
questions.explanation: Apsara's enduring significance is as Asia's first nuclear research reactor and a cornerstone of India's nuclear science and training programme.
